# RouteMIDI

> RouteMIDI is a multi-platform (macOS, Windows, Linux) command-line tool that
> routes MIDI messages between ports while filtering, transforming and
> converting them. It is the routing/processing member of a family of CLI MIDI
> tools alongside SendMIDI (send) and ReceiveMIDI (receive/monitor), and all
> three share the same text MIDI format, so they compose with Unix pipes.

## Generation contract

Rules for generating correct RouteMIDI command lines.

**Machine discovery** (experimental and fast-moving: the JSON shapes, MCP tool
names and protocol version may change between RouteMIDI releases)

- `routemidi --schema json` prints machine-readable command metadata and exits.
  The document includes `"experimental": true`.
- `routemidi --mcp` runs a stdio MCP server. MCP clients must be configured to
  launch this command; normal live routing commands do not advertise or expose
  an MCP endpoint.
- Once launched with `--mcp`, discover tools through MCP `initialize` and
  `tools/list`. The server exposes `get_schema`, `list_midi_ports`,
  `start_route`, `list_routes`, `inject_midi`, `add_commands`,
  `replace_command`, `remove_command`, `panic_route` and `stop_route`.
- `start_route` and `add_commands` take the same command tokens as the command
  line, minus the executable name, as a JSON array (one token per element). A
  good workflow: verify tokens with a one-shot `in - ... out -` text pipe, then
  start the identical tokens live. MCP mode allows routing (`in`, `out`,
  `vin`, `vout`, `panic`), number settings (`dec`, `hex`, `omc`) and every
  processing command; everything else is rejected: `-` routes and monitoring
  because MCP owns stdout for JSON-RPC framing, and scripting (`js`, `jsf`),
  file capture (`syf`) and program files (`file`) because they run code or
  touch the local file system. Invalid or
  partial commands reject atomically: on error nothing is started and session
  settings are rolled back.
- Routes have stable ids. `list_routes` reports each route's ports and staged
  commands with per-stage indexes; `add_commands`/`replace_command`/
  `remove_command` edit a running route live (e.g. change a scale or remap a
  curve), and `panic_route` releases anything left sounding after editing
  stateful commands such as latch, mono, the pedals or the MPE operations.
- `inject_midi` sends text MIDI messages (same format as below, one per array
  element) into a running route as if they arrived on one of its inputs: they
  run through the route's pipeline to its outputs, and the result echoes what
  the route emitted (with the output ports each message went to), so a live
  route can be auditioned and verified in place. Messages send immediately,
  in order, without scheduling, at most 128 per call. Parsing is permissive:
  a missing trailing value defaults to 0 (a velocity-less note-on is an
  effective note-off), so write every operand and check the echo.

**Route-start rules**

- Invocation: `routemidi [settings] in <port> [commands ...] out <port> [in ... out ...]`
- Every route must start with `in` or `vin`; filter/transform/MPE/convert
  commands given before any `in` are ignored with a warning.
- More `in` commands before an `out` add inputs (merge); more `out` commands
  add outputs (split). The first `in` after an `out` starts a new, independent
  route. Every input of a route is forwarded to every output of that route.
- Port names match case-insensitively by substring; quote names with spaces.
  `-` as a port name is a text stream on stdin/stdout.
- Settings (`dec`, `hex`, `omc`, `mon`, `ts`, `nn`, `src`, `panic`) may appear
  anywhere.

**Command ordering rules**

- Messages flow through fixed stages regardless of where commands are written:
  1 filters, 2 transforms (in written order), 3 MPE operations (in written
  order), 4 conversions, 5 outputs. A `transp` always runs before an `mpemono`,
  even when written after it.
- Within the transform stage order matters and composes: `chord 4 7 scale C
  major` stacks a triad then snaps it into the key.
- Filters: none = everything passes; one or more positive filters form a
  whitelist; `not <filter>` blocks matches and negates only the next filter.

**Optional argument traps**

- Optional arguments (`on`/`off`/`pp`/`cc`/`cc14`/`nrpn`/`rpn`/`pc` selectors, `latch` mode,
  `mono` priority, `vin`/`vout` names, `mpesplit` channel) end at the first
  token that spells a RouteMIDI command name: `on clock` is a note-on filter
  with NO note plus a clock filter, not "note-on for a note called clock".
  Never rely on a value or port name that spells a command word in an optional
  position.
- Fixed arguments are the opposite: they are taken literally even when they
  spell a command name (`in cc` names a port "cc").
- `convert` is variable-length (`srctype [num] dsttype [num]`): `cc`, `cc14`,
  `rpn`, `nrpn` need a number; `pb`, `cp`, `pc` take none; `pp` needs a note as
  a destination but may omit it as a source (meaning "any note", collapsed
  with the maximum of the held notes).
- Ranges are written `lo..hi` (never `lo-hi`, which clashes with note names
  like `C-2`). Numbers are decimal by default; a `H`/`M` suffix forces
  hex/decimal per number. Channels are 1-16, notes 0-127 or names `C-2`..`G8`
  (middle C = `C3`), MPE zones `lower`/`upper[:members]`.

**Text MIDI examples**

One message per line, an optional `channel <1-16>` prefix, then the type:

```
channel 1 note-on C3 100
channel 1 note-off 60 0
channel 2 control-change 74 64
channel 1 cc14 7 8000
channel 1 pitch-bend 8192
channel 1 poly-pressure C3 90
midi-clock
system-exclusive hex 7E 7F 06 01
```

Any pipeline can be verified without MIDI hardware:

```
$ printf 'channel 1 note-on 60 100\n' | routemidi in - transp 12 out -
channel  1   note-on           C4 100
```

**Safe live use**

- Add `panic` to live-performance routes: it sends all-notes-off when an input
  disconnects, when RouteMIDI exits, and when an MPE zone is reconfigured,
  preventing stuck notes.
- Positive filters are whitelists: `note` alone silently drops clock, transport
  and everything else. If the destination needs clock, include `sr` or `clock`
  in the whitelist. When merging several inputs, keep clock from only one of
  them (route the others through `not sr`) or the tempo messages double up.
- `js` scripts run synchronously on the routing path for every message: keep
  them short, never call `Util.sleep` in a live route, and remember that
  `MIDI.send*()` emits immediately and script global state persists across
  messages.

## Docs

- [README](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/gbevin/RouteMIDI/master/README.md): the full manual: every command, filter semantics, transforms, MPE routing, conversions and the text MIDI format
- [AI.md](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/gbevin/RouteMIDI/master/AI.md): driving RouteMIDI from AI tools and scripts: the generation contract, the `--schema json` metadata, the `--mcp` MCP server and its security model (experimental and fast-moving)
- [JAVASCRIPT.md](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/gbevin/RouteMIDI/master/JAVASCRIPT.md): the per-message JavaScript API (accessors, send functions, OSC, persistent state)

## Related

- [SendMIDI](https://github.com/gbevin/SendMIDI): send MIDI messages from the command line
- [ReceiveMIDI](https://github.com/gbevin/ReceiveMIDI): receive and monitor MIDI messages from the command line
